settings.title: A Wildflower Meadow on an Old Paddock
settings.summary: We turned a half-acre of rough paddock into a managed wildflower meadow with mown paths.
settings.description:
The owners of a smallholding near Tarrant Hinton had half an acre of coarse grass doing nothing and wanted more wildlife without a manicured look. They were happy for it to feel wild, as long as it did not just turn into a tangle of nettles and dock.

The challenge of an existing sward

The hardest part of making a meadow on old paddock is the grass that is already there. Vigorous turf shades out wildflower seedlings before they get going, so we could not simply scatter seed and hope. The ground also held plenty of nutrients from years as grazing, which favours grass over flowers.

What we did

We stripped out the most vigorous turf to open up bare ground and knock back the grass's head start. In autumn we sowed a mix built around yellow rattle, which weakens the grasses and lets the flowers compete, alongside a range of native perennials suited to the soil.

So the family could enjoy the space straight away, we cut winding paths through the sward. The mown routes give a deliberate edge to the wildness and make the meadow feel cared for rather than abandoned.

  • Vigorous turf stripped to open up bare ground
  • Autumn sowing of yellow rattle and a native perennial mix
  • Winding mown paths cut through the meadow

The first full summer

By the second June it was full of oxeye daisies, knapweed, and field scabious, with butterflies and grasshoppers that were not there before. One cut and rake each September keeps it going, removing the cut material so the soil slowly gets leaner and the flowers keep the upper hand.
